title: Fainting with black stools

chart.oneLiner: 70M presents after fainting at home; he has felt increasingly tired and lightheaded and takes daily NSAIDs for joint pain.

opening: I stood up, went lightheaded, and the next thing I knew I was on the floor.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 70-year-old man, pale and tired-looking. Good historian once prompted.

HPI: Several days of fatigue and lightheadedness, today a syncopal episode when standing. On asking: he has noticed BLACK, TARRY stools for a few days (melena) and mild epigastric discomfort. Takes ibuprofen daily for knee arthritis and is on aspirin. NO frank hematemesis, NO chest pain, NO palpitations. The faint was preceded by lightheadedness on standing (hypovolemic).
PMH: osteoarthritis, hypertension, coronary disease. MEDS: daily ibuprofen, aspirin, lisinopril. SOCIAL: occasional alcohol.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale, mildly tachycardic, looks fatigued.
- Vitals: BP 102/64, HR 108, RR 18, 36.9°C, SpO2 98% RA; orthostatic drop if checked.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness, no peritonism.
- RECTAL EXAM: melena (black tarry stool) on the glove if performed — the key finding.
- Conjunctival pallor if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the black stools, NSAID/aspirin use, or epigastric discomfort unless asked about stool color/GI symptoms, medications/pain relievers, or abdominal symptoms.
- The melena is revealed only if the student performs (or asks for) a rectal exam.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: Hgb 7.6 (low), MCV low-normal, WBC 8.0, Plt 280 — significant anemia.
labs.BMP: BUN 42, Cr 1.1 — elevated BUN:Cr ratio consistent with an upper GI bleed.
labs.StoolEvaluation: Melena (black, tarry stool) is documented on the chart — indicates a GI bleeding source. Provided as a clinical/diagnostic finding; no sensitive exam is required of the student.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Tall peaked symmetric T waves, flattening/loss of P waves, broadening QRS.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Tall peaked symmetric T waves, flattening/loss of P waves, broadening QRS. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Healed/healing rib fractures with callus along the lateral ribs; lungs clear.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Healed/healing rib fractures with callus along the lateral ribs; lungs clear. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing old healed rib fractures

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for the syncope (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Hypovolemic syncope from acute GI blood loss (leading & CANT-MISS,8). Orthostatic hypotension (core,3). Cardiac arrhythmia (CANT-MISS,4). Anemia of other cause (core,3). Vasovagal (less likely) (core,2). Connects pallor + tachycardia + (on history) melena/NSAID use to occult GI bleeding (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Hypovolemic syncope from acute GI blood loss. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Cardiac arrhythmia. Also reasonable: Orthostatic hypotension; Anemia of other cause; Vasovagal (less likely).

criticalActions.item: Hypovolemic syncope from acute GI blood loss

coreActions.item: Connects pallor + tachycardia + (on history) melena/NSAID use to occult GI bleeding

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order? (Don't forget the bedside.)
steps.rubric: CBC for anemia (CANT-MISS,4). Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena (CANT-MISS,4). BMP — note BUN:Cr ratio (2). Type and crossmatch (2). Coagulation studies (1). EKG (1). Orthostatic vitals (1). PENALTY: working up syncope without a rectal exam / not checking a hemoglobin (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: CBC for anemia; Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena

penalties.item: working up syncope without a rectal exam / not checking a hemoglobin

requiredTests: Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena
contraindicatedTests: working up syncope without a rectal exam / not checking a hemoglobin
orderSensitiveActions: working up syncope without a rectal exam / not checking a hemoglobin

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, possible demand-related ST changes from anemia, no STEMI. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (6); recognizes any demand ST changes are secondary to anemia, not primary ACS (5); excludes STEMI (2); systematic read (2). PENALTY: calling a primary STEMI (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes a hyperkalemia pattern as urgent
criticalFindings: Hyperkalemia pattern: peaked T waves with QRS widening.
commonMistakes: dismissing peaked T waves as a normal variant
missing the loss of P waves
steps.idealAnswer: Hyperkalemia: tall peaked T waves, P-wave flattening, widening QRS — a time-critical pattern.

criticalActions: Hyperkalemia pattern: peaked T waves with QRS widening.
coreActions: recognizes a hyperkalemia pattern as urgent

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes low yield here (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: identifies healed rib fractures with callus
commonMistakes: mistaking callus for a pulmonary or pleural lesion
steps.idealAnswer: Old healed rib fractures with bony callus along the lateral ribs.

coreActions: identifies healed rib fractures with callus

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Hgb is 7.6 with an elevated BUN:Cr and melena on rectal exam. What is your diagnosis and reasoning?
steps.rubric: Syncope from an acute (likely upper) GI bleed causing hypovolemia/anemia (6). Cites melena + elevated BUN:Cr + NSAID/aspirin use as the source (4).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH GI bleed: resuscitate – 2 large-bore IVs + crystalloid + type & screen (CANT-MISS,6); transfuse restrictive Hgb>7 (>8 if CAD) (CANT-MISS,5); GI consult for endoscopy to localize/treat (5); IV PPI (3); correct coagulopathy / hold anticoagulants (3); serial Hgb monitoring (2). PENALTY: attributing syncope to vasovagal and discharging without recognizing the bleed (-4); over-transfusion (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Syncope from occult GI bleed: resuscitate – 2 large-bore IVs, isotonic crystalloid, type & screen, transfuse pRBCs to a restrictive threshold (Hgb >7, or >8 if CAD). NPO. IV PPI. GI consult for endoscopy (EGD; colonoscopy if lower source) to localize and treat. Correct coagulopathy; hold/reverse anticoagulants. Serial Hgb (lags 24–72 h).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 70M presents after fainting at home; he has felt increasingly tired and lightheaded and takes daily NSAIDs for joint pain. The diagnosis is Syncope from occult GI bleed, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness, no peritonism.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness, no peritonism.
Vitals: BP 102/64, HR 108, RR 18, 36.9°C, SpO2 98% RA; orthostatic drop if checked.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Syncope from occult GI bleed while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Syncope from occult GI bleed and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalWorkupItems: Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia; possible demand-related ST changes given anemia; no acute STEMI.
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Hyperkalemia pattern: peaked T waves with QRS widening.

imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
